title: A New Force Posture Concept for Europeanizing Extended Nuclear Deterrence
gist: A former senior U.S. arms-control official argues that France's new 'forward deterrence' doctrine cannot underpin extended deterrence for NATO, and proposes a low-cost workaround: a modular small nuclear warhead ('PUMA-C') that can be dropped into Europe's existing and planned conventional long-range missiles, turning the continent's missile renaissance into a dispersed theater-nuclear deterrent against Russia.

insights.claim: France's 'dissuasion avancee' (forward deterrence) only improves the survivability of its airborne nuclear forces during crises and is incapable of underpinning extended deterrence for the NATO alliance.
insights.speaker: Christopher Ford
insights.so_what: European 'nuclear umbrella' optimism around Macron's policy is overstated; a real Euro-deterrent gap persists that drives defense and proliferation spending.
insights.quote: this dissuasion avancee is incapable of underpinning extended deterrence for the NATO alliance.
insights.locator: Nor is this merely a problem of intentions

insights.claim: France's nuclear doctrine explicitly rejects flexible response and treats nuclear use as binary and exclusively strategic, leaving it with no graduated options for theater-level crises.
insights.speaker: Christopher Ford
insights.so_what: An all-or-nothing French posture means NATO still lacks a credible mid-rung response to limited Russian nuclear coercion.
insights.quote: rejects the idea of flexible nuclear response. French nuclear capabilities are strategic and exclusively strategic
insights.locator: On the matter of doctrinal flexibility

insights.claim: France's air-launched cruise missile force is too small (about 50 warheads, split land- and carrier-based) and too high-yield (150-300 kilotons) to manage escalation, suiting only countervalue threats against Russian cities.
insights.speaker: Christopher Ford
insights.so_what: Quantifies the European theater-nuclear shortfall that any 'Europeanization' must fill; sets a baseline for procurement.
insights.quote: France has deployed only about 50 of them, divided between land-based and carrier-based aircraft.
insights.locator: Worse still, very few such French missiles

insights.claim: France refuses to participate in the NATO Nuclear Planning Group even as it disperses nuclear strike aircraft to NATO bases, which may make alliance nuclear planning less flexible rather than more.
insights.speaker: Christopher Ford
insights.so_what: Institutional friction limits how much Franco-NATO nuclear integration can actually deliver despite the headline dispersal policy.
insights.locator: If anything, this new French policy may, in practice

insights.claim: Ford proposes a 'potentially ubiquitous modular aerial capability' (PUMA-C): a small nuclear warhead designed to drop into the existing conventional long-range missiles Europe already fields, avoiding a bespoke nuclear-only delivery system.
insights.speaker: Christopher Ford
insights.so_what: If pursued, it would convert European conventional-missile procurement budgets into latent nuclear delivery capacity, reshaping the strike-missile market.
insights.quote: I call this idea “potentially ubiquitous modular aerial capability” (those who like acronyms can call it the PUMA-C for short).
insights.locator: Potentially Ubiquitous Modular Aerial Capability

insights.claim: Europe is undergoing a 'missile renaissance' under the European Long-Range Strike Approach, expected to produce a ground-launched cruise missile with a range of 1,000-2,000 kilometers.
insights.speaker: Christopher Ford
insights.so_what: Flags a concrete European long-range missile build-out that defense suppliers and investors should track as a growth program.
insights.quote: a ground-launched cruise missile with a range of between 1,000 to 2,000 kilometres
insights.locator: Indeed, Europe is presently said to be undergoing

insights.claim: Developing a small nuclear warhead for existing missiles could save many billions of euros versus building new nuclear-only systems, since delivery-system development has historically cost more than the warhead itself.
insights.speaker: Christopher Ford
insights.so_what: The cost asymmetry is the core economic argument for reusing conventional missiles as nuclear carriers.
insights.quote: the development of which cost more than the Manhattan Project itself.
insights.locator: Historically, it is not at all unprecedented

insights.claim: Existing European and allied missiles such as JASSM, Storm Shadow, Tomahawk, and the Turkish Bora form a sizeable arsenal that could in principle carry an optional small nuclear payload.
insights.speaker: Christopher Ford
insights.so_what: Identifies the specific platforms whose dual-capability would expand if the concept advances.
insights.locator: Even without new long-range systems

insights.claim: The hardest engineering problem is not the warhead but a 'plug-and-play' arming, fusing, and firing system that can electronically interface with the diverse guidance computers of many different missiles.
insights.speaker: Christopher Ford
insights.so_what: Points to where R&D risk and contracting value would actually concentrate if the program were funded.
insights.quote: developing a sufficiently clever arming, fusing, and firing system
insights.locator: In fact, the most technically challenging part

insights.claim: A pre-tested low-yield design such as a new British WE-177 variant (0.5-450 kilotons) or the French AN-51 (10-25 kilotons) could be adapted with more confidence than an entirely new untested device.
insights.speaker: Christopher Ford
insights.so_what: Existing tested designs lower the technical and political barrier to a no-test Euro warhead.
insights.locator: A new version of the old British WE-177 gravity bomb

insights.claim: Expanding European nuclear capacity would require more plutonium pits, and pit production is very hard to scale because Britain, France, and the U.S. have all 'hard-wired' themselves into extremely low weapons-production capability.
insights.speaker: Christopher Ford
insights.so_what: Pit-production bottlenecks are the binding industrial constraint on any Western theater-nuclear expansion.
insights.quote: pit production is not easy to expand, and this is something we in the United States struggle with to this day.
insights.locator: For one thing, it would be necessary to produce more plutonium

insights.claim: France already holds over 100 tons of plutonium, mostly from spent reactor fuel, so material supply is not the limiting factor.
insights.speaker: Christopher Ford
insights.so_what: The constraint is processing/pit capacity, not fissile stock, narrowing where investment is needed.
insights.quote: reportedly now having over 100 tons of such material, mostly from spent reactor fuel.
insights.locator: France doesn’t lack plutonium

insights.claim: Russia fields a 'Swiss Army knife' arsenal of theater nuclear tools giving it many escalation rungs, while NATO has very few, an asymmetry that invites Russian coercive bargaining or war.
insights.speaker: Christopher Ford
insights.so_what: Frames the deterrence-gap thesis driving European and U.S. theater-nuclear procurement debates.
insights.quote: a ‘Swiss Army knife’ arsenal that provides a nuclear tool for nearly any occasion, large or small.
insights.locator: in what I have called

insights.claim: Putin proclaimed himself 'ready right now' for war with Europe and relies on the strategic nuclear balance with the U.S. as an 'offensive umbrella' to deter NATO from intervening against Russian aggression.
insights.speaker: Christopher Ford
insights.so_what: The cited Russian strategy underlies elevated European defense spending and the deterrence-gap narrative.
insights.quote: the “offensive umbrella” of its strategic nuclear balance with the United States
insights.locator: In this strategy, the Kremlin trusts

insights.claim: NATO's only theater-class nuclear options today are a small number of American gravity bombs deliverable by vulnerable aircraft and a few low-yield W76-2 warheads riding strategic submarine missiles, leaving its response 'disturbingly binary.'
insights.speaker: Christopher Ford
insights.so_what: Defines the capability hole PUMA-C aims to fill and the current vulnerability of NATO's nuclear posture.
insights.quote: NATO’s nuclear options in the face of such coercive nuclear bargaining would be disturbingly binary
insights.locator: Having no meaningful theater-class nuclear capabilities

insights.claim: The entanglement critique of dual-capable missiles has aged poorly: Russia has fired a wide array of dual-capable missiles at Ukraine without anyone concluding nuclear war was underway.
insights.speaker: Christopher Ford
insights.so_what: Uses Ukraine-war empirics to rebut the main stability objection to Western dual-capable systems.
insights.quote: without anyone there or elsewhere concluding that a nuclear war was getting underway
insights.locator: Russia, after all, has fired a bewildering array

insights.claim: Asymmetric entanglement is more destabilizing than mutual entanglement, because Russia and China deploy dual-capable systems to self-deter Western strikes while feeling free to attack non-dual-capable Western systems.
insights.speaker: Christopher Ford
insights.so_what: Reframes dual-capability as a way to correct a strategic disadvantage, not create one.
insights.locator: Asymmetric entanglement, I fear

insights.claim: Dispersing nuclear-capable missiles 'everywhere' across the alliance would create deterrence by denial, making Russian preemption infeasible and therefore less likely to be attempted.
insights.speaker: Christopher Ford
insights.so_what: Ubiquity-as-survivability is the structural advantage claimed for the concept over today's concentrated B61 storage.
insights.quote: for preemption, which cannot feasibly succeed, is preemption presumably less likely to be undertaken.
insights.locator: This might thus also bring about some degree

insights.claim: Ford argues PUMA-C would not be a proliferation problem because it involves only French or British weapons, and could even reduce proliferation pressure on other NATO members the way nuclear sharing historically has.
insights.speaker: Christopher Ford
insights.so_what: Preempts the legal objection and positions the scheme as nonproliferation-positive to ease political adoption.
insights.locator: Nor would this represent a nuclear proliferation problem

insights.claim: The strongest case for the concept is the difficulty or impossibility of meeting current deterrence needs by any less radical means amid growing American ambivalence about Europe.
insights.speaker: Christopher Ford
insights.so_what: Signals expectation of weakening U.S. commitment to NATO, a structural driver of European strategic autonomy spending.
insights.quote: the difficulty, or perhaps the impossibility, of meeting current security threats and deterrence needs by any less radical means.
insights.locator: Most importantly, however
